steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the underlined sentence, which presents The Times's view that everyone should be free to look and act however they like. The word "But" opens the very next sentence, signaling that Arnold immediately contradicts this view. The underlined sentence sets up the position he will reject.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "It presents an opinion with which the author disagrees.": Arnold uses The Times's position only to refute it — "But culture indefatigably tries" to move people toward refined taste instead of letting each raw person do as he likes. The underlined sentence is the position Arnold argues against. ✓
  • "It asserts that the English are not as well known for their sense of taste as they ought to be.": The sentence says nothing about how the English are known internationally; it simply states The Times's view on individual freedom. This detail is invented. ✗
  • "It implies that opinions regarding culture evolve over time.": Nothing in the sentence or the passage suggests opinions change over time. The contrast is between two simultaneous views, not an evolution. ✗
  • "It details an example that supports the author's primary claim.": The sentence opposes Arnold's claim rather than supporting it — he argues culture should refine taste, while The Times argues for freedom from such standards. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
